text: We present a case of a laparoscopic repair of a left psoas hernia with mesh.

The patient is a twenty four-year-old male presenting with a several week history of lifestyle limiting left abdominal and left flank pain. The pain was exacerbated by movement. He had no past medical or surgical history. His physical exam was normal showing no hernias or other abnormalities.

A CT scan was obtained to further evaluate his pain. CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed a psoas hernia with his descending colon lying posterior to his left psoas muscle. There were no signs of colon obstruction. The CT was otherwise normal.
Given no other findings for his significant pain, a laparoscopic repair of the psoas hernia was recommended.

The patient is positioned in the right lateral decubitus position. The surgeon accesses the abdomen using a paraumbilical Hassan entry technique, followed by the placement of two additional 5mm laparoscopic ports slightly left of midline in the subxiphoid and lower midline areas.

The laparoscopic view of the abdomen initially shows the prominent left psoas muscle obstructing the descending colon. With retraction, the descending colon is elevated from its position posterior to the psoas muscle. The surgeon dissects along the white line of Toldt to medialize the descending colon, without mobilizing the splenic flexure.
The surgeon dissects the peritoneum of the left lateral abdominal wall, carefully exposing the course of the nerves to avoid injury during the mesh fixation process.

A 15x15 cm polyester composite mesh is positioned over the hernia defect. The mesh is secured to the lateral abdominal wall beneath the peritoneum using absorbable tacks. Medially, it is attached to the anterior psoas and dorotus fascia with fibrin sealant, ensuring the ureter remains uncovered.
The surgeon repositions the descending colon back to its anatomical location after previously elevating it from behind the left psoas muscle.
The patient was discharged to home the day of surgery. At follow-up, he reported resolution of his preoperative symptoms and had returned to normal activity.
